10: elthen gar ho huios tou anthropou zetesai kai sosai to apololes — for the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost — Luke's mission-statement (parallel to Matthew 20:28 / Mark 10:45 but using sozo and apollymi instead of the ransom-language). Zetesai (to seek) echoes the seeking-shepherd of Ezekiel 34:11 and the seeking-woman of Luke 15:8; sosai (to save) is Luke's characteristic salvation-vocabulary; to apololes (the lost thing, the perishing one) echoes all three parables of Luke 15 (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son). Zacchaeus is the concrete instance of the Son of Man's seeking-and-saving mission.
1: Kai eiselthōn diērcheto tēn Iericho — entering, he was passing through Jericho — Iericho (Jericho) was the final major stop on the ascent road from the Jordan valley to Jerusalem; ~27km separated it from Jerusalem, with a 1,000m altitude gain. Eiselthōn diērcheto (entering, was passing through — two verbs indicating momentary entry into a through-passage) sets up the dramatic interruption: Jesus was not stopping in Jericho but is detained by two encounters (blind Bartimaeus in Luke 18:35-43; Zacchaeus here). The Jericho scenes are Luke's final station before the Jerusalem entry.
2: kai idou anēr onomati kaloumenos Zakchaios, kai autos ēn architelōnēs kai autos plousios — and there was a man called by name Zacchaeus, and he was a chief tax collector and wealthy — Zakchaios is the Greek form of the Hebrew Zakkay (pure, innocent — a name whose irony Luke's audience would appreciate). Architelōnēs (chief tax collector — a compound unique to Luke in the NT; archi- = head, chief + telōnēs = tax collector) implies a supervisory role over a revenue district, not merely a single customs booth. Jericho was a wealthy customs location on the balsam-trade route; plousios (wealthy) is the third descriptor, completing his social profile: named, powerful, rich.
3: kai ezētei idein ton Iēsoun tis estin kai ouk ēdynato apo tou ochlou, hoti tē hēlikia mikros ēn — he was seeking to see who Jesus was and could not because of the crowd, because he was small in stature — ezētei idein (was seeking to see — imperfect of persistent effort) before the encounter begins. Tis estin (who he is) — the same Christological question the storm disciples asked (Luke 8:25); Zacchaeus is asking the right question. Tē hēlikia mikros (small in stature — hēlikia can mean age or stature; here physical height, confirmed by Luke 19:4). The crowd that surrounds Jesus is the immediate obstacle; Zacchaeus's social marginality (tax collectors were despised) may have meant no one would let him through.
4: kai prodramōn eis to emprosthen anebē epi sykomorean — running ahead to the front, he climbed a sycamore-fig tree — prodramōn (running ahead — aorist participle of proactive initiative; an undignified act for a wealthy official in honor-based Mediterranean culture). Sykomorea (sycamore-fig — Ficus sycomorus, different from the sycamore of temperate climates; a spreading tree with low branches accessible for climbing, common in the Jericho valley). The wealthy tax official climbing a tree to see Jesus inverts the expected social hierarchy: the powerful man humbles himself to a childlike position. hina idē auton, hoti ekeinēs emellen diercheisthai — to see him because he was about to pass through that way.
5: kai hōs ēlthen epi ton topon, anablepsas ho Iēsous eipen pros auton, Zakchaie, speusas kataba — when he came to the place, looking up Jesus said to him: Zacchaeus, hurry and come down — Jesus looks up (anablepsas — the verb for restored sight in Luke 18:41-43; the Bartimaeus healing is still fresh in the narrative) and addresses Zacchaeus by name: divine initiative from below. sēmeron gar en tō oikō sou dei me meinai — for today it is necessary for me to remain in your house — dei (divine necessity — Luke's compulsion word) — the stay in Zacchaeus's house is not social preference but divine appointment. Sēmeron (today — Luke's eschatological present; cf. Luke 4:21; Luke 23:43) marks the moment of salvation's arrival.
6: kai speusas katebē kai hypedexato auton chairōn — hurrying he came down and welcomed him rejoicing — the speed and joy of the response: speusas (hurrying — the same root as the angels' urgency in Luke 2:16; the shepherds who "went with haste") and chairōn (rejoicing — the characteristic Lukan response to grace). Hypedechomai (to welcome, receive as a guest — a compound of dechomai: to accept, receive; the posture of hospitality and humble reception) — Zacchaeus is not a passive recipient but an active welcomer. The reception mirrors the prodigal's father (Luke 15:20) and the host who runs to meet the returning son.
7: kai idontes pantes diegoggyzon legontes hoti Para hamartōlō andri eisēlthen katalysai — all seeing it were murmuring, saying: he has gone in to stay with a sinful man — diegoggyzon (were murmuring, grumbling thoroughly — the dia-prefix intensifies; the LXX term for Israel's wilderness complaints, cf. Luke 5:30) from the entire crowd. Hamartōlō andri (a sinful man) — the social verdict on the tax collector; he is categorically excluded from table-fellowship for those maintaining purity. The crowd's grumble is the same as the Pharisees' in Luke 5:30 ("Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?") — the question the next three verses will answer definitively.
8: statheis de Zakchaios eipen pros ton Kyrion, Idou ta hēmisē mou tōn hyparchontōn, Kyrie, tois ptōchois didōmi — Zacchaeus stood and said to the Lord: behold, half of my possessions, Lord, I give to the poor — statheis (standing — taking a public, formal stance) marks a public declaration. Ta hēmisē (half — not all, which would be poverty, but the substantial half, exceeding the legal restitution requirement). The present tense didōmi (I give — not "I will give") indicates a current practice or an immediate decision, not a future promise. kai ei tinos ti esykophantēsa, apodidōmi tetraploun — and if I defrauded anyone of anything, I repay fourfold — sykophantō (to defraud, extort — cf. Luke 3:14) followed by fourfold restitution exceeds the Torah's requirement (Exodus 22:1 four-fold for stolen sheep; Leviticus 6:5 requires 100% + 20%).
9: eipen de pros auton ho Iēsous, Sēmeron sōtēria tō oikō toutō egeneto — Jesus said to him: today salvation has come to this house — Sēmeron sōtēria egeneto (today salvation happened/came into existence — aorist of accomplished fact) — sōtēria (salvation) has arrived in the person of Jesus; it came with him when he entered. Tō oikō toutō (to this house) — household-inclusive salvation: Zacchaeus's whole household is encompassed (cf. Acts 16:31; 18:8 where household salvation becomes normative in Acts). kathoti kai autos huios Abraam estin — because he also is a son of Abraham — the "son of Abraham" declaration (cf. Luke 13:16 "daughter of Abraham") restores Zacchaeus's covenant status that his profession had forfeited in popular estimation; the declaration is divine, not social.
11: Akouontōn de autōn tauta prostheis eipen parabolēn — as they were hearing these things he added a parable — the parable of the Pounds is told because of a mistaken expectation: dia to engys einai Ierousalēm auton kai dokein autous hoti parachrēma mellei hē basileia tou Theou anaphainesthai — because he was near Jerusalem and they thought the kingdom of God was going to appear immediately — parachrēma (immediately, at once — Luke's healing-stamp word applied here to eschatological expectation) captures the disciples' imminent-kingdom excitement. The parable corrects this: there is an intervening period (the nobleman going to a distant country) before the kingdom's full arrival.
12: eipen oun, Anthrōpos tis eugenēs eporeuthē eis chōran makran labein heautō basileian kai hypostrephai — a man of noble birth went to a distant country to receive for himself a kingdom and to return — eugenēs (well-born, of noble lineage — the aristocratic qualifier) and the journey to receive a kingdom in a distant land evokes the historical precedent of Archelaus, who in 4 BCE traveled to Rome to receive confirmation of his inheritance of Judea from Caesar Augustus (Josephus, Ant. 17.299-303). A delegation of Jews had petitioned against him (Josephus, Ant. 17.300) — precisely the pattern of Luke 19:14. The allegory maps the absent nobleman onto Jesus's ascension and return.
13: kalesas de deka doulous heautou edōken autois deka mnas kai eipen pros autous, Pragmateusasthe en hō erchomai — calling ten of his servants he gave them ten minas and said to them: do business until I come — mna (mina — a Greek monetary unit = 100 drachmas = ~3.3 months' wages for a laborer; vs. Matthew's talanta which were 60 minas each). Ten servants receive one mina each — a modest but real commission. Pragmateuomai (to do business, trade, engage in commerce) is a commercial term for active investment. The instruction "until I come" (en hō erchomai) marks the period of the absent king as the time for faithful stewardship — an ecclesiological comment on the church's mandate in the interim.
14: hoi de politai autou emisoun auton kai apesteilan presbeiav opisō autou legontes, Ou thelomen touton basileusai eph hēmas — but his citizens hated him and sent a delegation after him saying: we do not want this man to reign over us — emisoun (were hating — imperfect of continuous hatred), presbeia (a delegation, embassy — the formal ambassadorial term for an official delegation sent to a higher authority). The historical parallel with the anti-Archelaus delegation is exact (Josephus, Ant. 17.300: a delegation of fifty Jews sent to Augustus). In the parable's allegory, the rejection of the nobleman is the rejection of Jesus by the Jerusalem establishment, climaxing in the Passion.
15: kai egeneto en tō epanelthein auton labonta tēn basileian kai eipen phōnēthēnai autō tous doulous toutous — when he returned having received the kingdom, he commanded that these servants be called — the return completes the allegory of the ascension-and-parousia: the nobleman (Jesus) has received the kingdom (exaltation to God's right hand) and returns to reckon with his servants. hina gnoi ti diepragmateusanto — in order to know what business they had conducted — diapragmateuomai (to conduct business thoroughly, trade diligently — compound of dia + pragmateuomai) is the accounting moment: what the servants did with the commission during the absent period.
16: parGeneto de ho prōtos legōn, Kyrie, hē mna sou deka mnas prosērgasato — the first came saying: Lord, your mina has earned ten minas more — prosērgasato (earned in addition, produced as a surplus — from prosergazomai: to work toward in addition; a commercial production term). Ten-fold return on a single mina is exceptional; the servant's use of "your mina" (hē mna sou) is the key theological detail — the servant acknowledges the capital as belonging to the master, not himself. The faithful steward returns the master's investment with return, not the investment alone.
17: kai eipen autō, Euge, agathe doule, hoti en elachistō pistos egenou, isthi exousian echōn epanō deka poleōn — well done, good servant! because you were faithful in a very small matter, be having authority over ten cities — Euge (well done! — a Greek exclamation of approval; Matthew's parallel has "well done" differently). Elachistō (the least, smallest thing — a mina relative to a kingdom is elachiston) — the faithful management of a small commission earns authority over ten cities. The scale of reward exceeds the scale of the commission: faithful stewardship in the interim period earns eschatological authority in the kingdom. The reward is increased responsibility, not rest.
18: kai ēlthen ho deuteros legōn, Hē mna sou, Kyrie, epoiēsen pente mnas — the second came saying: your mina, Lord, has made five minas — the second servant's return is proportionally strong (5x) though less than the first (10x). Like the first, he uses "your mina" — acknowledging the master's ownership. Luke does not record the same verbal commendation as Matthew's parallel (Matthew 25:23 "well done") but the reward follows the same proportional logic.
19: eipen de kai toutō, Kai sy epanō ginou pente poleōn — he said also to this one: you also take charge of five cities — five cities for five-mina profit: the reward is proportional to return. The pattern of proportional reward (10 cities for 10 minas, 5 cities for 5 minas) establishes the kingdom's economy of faithfulness: the master does not flatten outcomes but calibrates recognition to the servant's results. The political-administrative character of the reward (governing cities) points to the disciples' eschatological role in the renewed creation (cf. Matthew 19:28; Revelation 20:4-6).
20: kai ho heteros ēlthen legōn, Kyrie, idou hē mna sou hēn eichon apokeimenēn en soudarion — the other came saying: Lord, behold your mina which I had stored in a cloth — apokeimenēn (stored away, laid up — perfect passive participle: kept in a fixed, unchanged state) — the mina returned is precisely the mina given; nothing gained, nothing lost. Soudariov (cloth, napkin — a Latin loanword from sudarium, a sweat cloth) was not a secure storage vessel; it suggests informal, low-security preservation rather than banking. The contrast with the first servants' active trading is stark: this servant did nothing with the commission.
21: ephoboumēn gar se, hoti anthrōpos austēros ei, aireis ho ouk ethēkas kai therizeis ho ouk espeiras — for I was afraid of you, because you are an exacting man; you take up what you did not set down and reap what you did not sow — austēros (austere, strict, exacting — from austēros meaning dry, harsh, stringent; used of a demanding master) is the servant's characterization of the master. The reaping-what-one-didn't-sow idiom describes appropriating others' labor — the servant's stated reason for non-investment is fear of the master's expectations, which ironically makes his inaction even more culpable: if the master is demanding, more reason to produce.
22: legei autō, Ek tou stomatos sou krinō se, ponēre doule — he says to him: from your own mouth I judge you, wicked servant — Ek tou stomatos sou krinō se (from your own mouth I judge you) — the servant's own stated beliefs about the master are turned into the basis of judgment. ēdeis hoti egō anthrōpos austēros eimi, airōn ho ouk ethēka kai therizōn ho ouk espeira — you knew that I am an exacting man, taking what I did not set down and reaping what I did not sow — if the servant's assessment of the master was correct, his failure to invest was the more inexcusable: the most exacting possible master would demand returns, not merely preservation of capital.
23: kai dia ti ouk edōkas mou to argyrion epi trapezan — and why therefore did you not give my money to the bank? — trapeza (table, banker's table — the money-changer's or banker's counter; the term for banking institutions in the Hellenistic world, cf. the temple money-changers' trapezai in Matthew 21:12). The minimum acceptable action was deposit for interest: even without active trading, the servant could have made something. kagō elthōn syn tokō an auto epraxa — and I, coming, would have collected it with interest — tokos (interest on a loan — literally "offspring," money that grows; cf. Exodus 22:25; Deuteronomy 23:19-20 which restricted interest among Israelites but not in commercial Gentile contexts) — even modest interest would have been better than no return.
24: kai tois parestōsin eipen, Arate ap autou tēn mnan kai dote tō tas deka mnas echonti — and to those standing by he said: take the mina from him and give it to the one who has ten minas — the redistribution is the application of the principle stated in Luke 19:26. Parestōsin (those standing by) — the bystanders at the judgment scene who carry out the king's orders. The mina taken from the unproductive servant goes to the most productive — not to the second servant with five but to the first with ten. The logic of kingdom economy: resources flow toward demonstrated fruitfulness, not toward potential or need.
25: kai eipan autō, Kyrie, echei deka mnas — they said to him: Lord, he already has ten minas — the objection of the bystanders is the expected human reaction: fairness would suggest giving to one with less, not more. The objection receives no rebuke but prompts the economic principle of Luke 19:26. Luke includes this exchange (absent from Matthew) to create space for the explicit principle-statement; the objection voices what the audience is thinking.
26: legō hymin hoti panti tō echonti dothēsetai, apo de tou mē echontos kai ho echei arthēsetai — I tell you that to everyone who has, more will be given; but from the one who does not have, even what he has will be taken away — the principle of kingdom economy (cf. Luke 8:18; Mark 4:25; Matthew 13:12; Luke 25:29): productive use of what is given generates increase; non-use leads to loss. The principle is not about initial endowment but about faithfulness with what one has been given. In the parable's context: those who stewarded the commission faithfully accumulate; those who did not lose even the original. The principle operates in the eschatological accounting, not in material economy.
27: plēn tous echthrous mou toutous tous mē thelēsantas me basileusai ep autous agagete hōde kai katasphaxate autous emprosthen mou — but as for these enemies of mine who did not want me to reign over them, bring them here and slaughter them before me — the king's command regarding the rebellious citizens (Luke 19:14) applies the political allegory to its historical conclusion: the anti-Archelaus delegation failed and Archelaus was confirmed; in the parable's deeper logic, this anticipates Jerusalem's destruction in 70 CE as the consequence of rejecting the Messiah-King. The violence of the image is the parable's realism about political consequences, not a pastoral instruction.
28: kai tauta eipōn eporeueto emprosthen anabainōn eis Ierousalēm — having said these things he was going ahead, going up to Jerusalem — emprosthen (ahead, in front) — Jesus leads the ascent; the disciples follow. Anabainōn eis Ierousalēm (going up to Jerusalem) — the geographical-theological ascent: from Jericho (-250m below sea level) to Jerusalem (+750m above sea level) is a 1,000m climb, always described as "going up" (anabainō). The journey narrative (Luke 9:51) that has been building since the Galilee chapters now reaches its destination: the next step after the parable is the entry into the city.
29: kai egeneto hōs ēggisen eis Bēthphagē kai Bēthanian pros to oros to kaloumenon Elaiōn — when he came near to Bethphage and Bethany at the mount called the Mount of Olives — Bēthphagē (Bethphage — "house of unripe figs," a village on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives) and Bēthanian (Bethany — the home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus; ~2km from Jerusalem). Oros ... Elaiōn (Mount of Olives) is the ridge east of Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley; Zechariah 14:4 places YHWH's feet there at the eschaton, giving the mountain eschatological significance. apesteilen dyo tōn mathētōn — he sent two of the disciples — the dual-witness commission (Deuteronomy 19:15).
30: Hypagete eis tēn katenanti kōmēn, en hē eisporeuomenoi heurēsete pōlon dedemenon, eph hon oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen — go into the village opposite, in which entering you will find a colt tied, on which no one has ever sat — pōlos (a young animal — in this context a young donkey; the LXX uses pōlos for the donkey's foal of Zechariah 9:9). Eph hon oudeis pōpote anthrōpōn ekathisen (on which no one has ever sat) — an unridden animal was considered sacred and appropriate for holy purposes (cf. Numbers 19:2 the unworked red heifer; 1 Samuel 6:7 the unworked cows). The unused animal is fitting for a king's ride. kai lysantes auton agagete — and untying him, bring him.
31: kai ean tis hymas erōtā, Dia ti lyete, houtōs ereite, Hoti ho Kyrios autou chreian echei — and if anyone asks you: why are you untying it? say thus: because the Lord needs it — ho Kyrios (the Lord) is used absolutely without further identification — the title the post-resurrection community used for the risen Jesus (cf. Acts 2:36). Chreian echei (has need, needs it) — the Lord's need is the authorization that overrides the owners' ordinary property rights. The pre-arranged answer (or a miraculous provision of the right words) demonstrates Jesus's sovereign knowledge of what will happen and his authority to commandeer what he needs for the messianic entry.
32: apelthontes de hoi apestalmenoi heuron kathōs eipen autois — going, those sent found it exactly as he had told them — kathōs eipen autois (just as he told them) is the verification of prophetic knowledge: Jesus's instruction was not a guess but foreknowledge. Luke uses this verification pattern in Luke 22:13 (the Passover room) to frame both the triumphal entry and the Last Supper as divinely arranged events. The disciples' experience of finding things "just as he said" is itself a discipleship formation: training them to trust Jesus's foreknowledge in preparation for the Passion's apparent chaos.
33: lyontōn de autōn ton pōlon eipan hoi kyrioi autou pros autous, Ti lyete ton pōlon — as they were untying the colt, its owners said to them: why are you untying the colt? — kyrioi autou (its owners — plural) — the colt has multiple owners (perhaps a family). The challenge is natural and expected; the disciples have been prepared for it (Luke 19:31). Luke's inclusion of the encounter makes the scene concrete: the disciples face an actual challenge and respond with the prepared answer. The owners' question is not hostile but naturally curious; what follows (Luke 19:34) is their simple acceptance of the answer.
34: hoi de eipan, Ho Kyrios autou chreian echei — they said: the Lord needs it — the identical phrase from Luke 19:31 is repeated verbatim: the prepared answer is given, word for word. Luke's repetition is narrative confirmation: what was predicted was enacted exactly. The owners' acceptance of this explanation without further question may indicate: (a) they knew Jesus, (b) they recognized a divine commission, or (c) Luke abbreviates their response. In any case, the colt is released; the messianic entry can proceed.
35: kai ēgagon auton pros ton Iēsoun kai epiriptes autōn ta himatia epi ton pōlon epebibasan ton Iēsoun — they brought it to Jesus and throwing their garments on the colt they set Jesus on it — epiriptes (throwing over, casting on) their garments (himatia) on the colt creates the impromptu saddle; the gesture is simultaneously practical and honorific. The placing of garments under a new king echoes 2 Kings 9:13 where the generals spread their garments under Jehu when proclaiming him king. Epebibasan (set him on it) — the disciples act as royal attendants placing the king on his mount.
36: poreuomenou de autou hypestrōnnyon ta himatia autōn en tē hodō — as he was going, they were spreading their garments on the road — hypestrōnnyon (were spreading under, strewing — imperfect of continuous action throughout the procession) the garments on the road — again the royal-installation gesture (2 Kings 9:13). Matthew (Luke 21:8) and Mark (Luke 11:8) add branches cut from trees; Luke mentions only garments, perhaps for literary simplicity or because branches were less common in the Jericho/Olivet corridor. The road-spreading creates a royal carpet for the Messiah-King's entrance.
37: eggizovtos de autou ēdē pros tē katabasei tou orous tōn Elaiōn ērxanto hapan to plēthos tōn mathētōn chairovtes ainein ton Theon phōnē megalē — as he was already approaching the descent of the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of disciples began rejoicing to praise God with a great voice — katabasis (descent, the downward slope) — on the descent from the Mount of Olives, Jerusalem comes into view across the Kidron; this is the moment of the crowd's eruption. Hapan to plēthos tōn mathētōn (the whole crowd of disciples — not just the Twelve but the wider disciple community) begins the acclamation. peri pasōn hōn eidon dynameōn — for all the works of power they had seen — the praise is grounded in witnessed miracles.
38: legontes, Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos ho basileus en onomati Kyriou — saying: blessed is the king who comes in the name of the Lord — Psalms 118:26 (LXX) is the citation (the final verse of the Hallel, sung at Passover). Luke uniquely inserts ho basileus (the King) into the Psalm citation, making the messianic application explicit. en ouranō eirēnē kai doxa en hypsistois — peace in heaven and glory in the highest — Luke's version replaces the crowd's "Hosanna" (Matthew, Mark, John) with a heavenly doxology that mirrors the birth announcement (Luke 2:14: "glory to God in the highest and on earth peace"). The triumphal entry announces the convergence of heaven and earth: peace that was "on earth" at the birth is now "in heaven" as the King approaches his throne through the cross.
39: kai tines tōn Pharisaiōn apo tou ochlou eipan pros auton, Didaskale, epitimēson tois mathētais sou — some of the Pharisees from the crowd said to him: Teacher, rebuke your disciples — the Pharisaic objection is to the messianic title "king" and the Hallel-citation; the messianic acclamation is publicly dangerous (Roman authorities would consider it seditious) and, in Pharisaic judgment, theologically inappropriate. Epitimēson tois mathētais sou (rebuke your disciples) — the same verb used for Jesus's rebukes of demons and fever; the Pharisees demand that Jesus silence the messianic proclamation.
40: kai apokritheis eipen, Legō hymin, ean houtoi siōpēsousin, hoi lithoi kraxousin — answering he said: I tell you, if these keep silent, the stones will cry out — the declaration is categorical: the messianic acclamation is so intrinsic to the moment that its suppression is cosmologically impossible. Hoi lithoi kraxousin (the stones will cry out) — an echo of Habakkuk 2:11 (the stones of unjust walls cry out) and the broader prophetic tradition of creation testifying to divine truth. The stones of Jerusalem — which in 40 years will be hurled down (Luke 19:44) — are here presented as potential proclaimers of the King.
41: kai hōs ēggisen, idōn tēn polin eklausen ep autēn — when he came near, seeing the city he wept over it — eklausen (he wept — aorist: a specific act of weeping, not merely watering eyes; the strong word for grief-weeping, used also for the widow of Nain 7:13 and Peter Luke 22:62). Luke is the only Gospel that records Jesus weeping over Jerusalem (John 11:35 records weeping over Lazarus; Mk/Mt have no parallel here). The weping occurs on the descent of the Mount of Olives as Jerusalem comes fully into view — the city of the temple, the city that kills prophets (Luke 13:34), the city he has been moving toward since Luke 9:51.
42: legōn hoti Ei egnōs en tē hēmera tautē kai sy ta pros eirēnēn — saying: if you had known, even you, in this day the things for peace — Ei egnōs ... ta pros eirēnēn (if you had known the things for peace — a second-class conditional expressing a contrary-to-fact situation: you did not know) — the lament over what might have been. Ta pros eirēnēn (the things for peace = what peace requires, the conditions of peace) — the shalom that the Messiah-King brings but Jerusalem cannot recognize. nyn de ekrybē apo ophthalmōn sou — but now it has been hidden from your eyes — ekrybē (was hidden — divine passive: God has hidden; the hardening of 8:10 applied nationally) — the opportunity has passed; the time of visitation (Luke 19:44) is closing.
43: hoti hēxousin hēmerai epi se kai parembalousin hoi echthroi sou charaka soi — because days will come upon you when your enemies will throw up a rampart against you — Jesus's prediction of Jerusalem's siege follows the prophetic pattern of Isaiah 29:3 (YHWH laying siege to Ariel), Jeremiah 6:6 (cutting down trees for siege-works), and especially Ezekiel 4:1-3 (the symbolic siege of Jerusalem). Charax (rampart, palisade of sharpened stakes — a Roman military term for circumvallation) describes precisely the siege techniques Titus used in 70 CE (Josephus, War 5.491-511): the circumvallation wall preventing escape. Perikyklōsousin se (will encircle you) is Josephus's exact language for Titus's siege.
44: kai edaphiousin se kai ta tekna sou en soi — they will dash you to the ground, you and your children within you — edaphizō (to raze to the ground, level — from edaphos, the floor or ground; a rare verb for total demolition) — Josephus describes the complete leveling of Jerusalem in 70 CE (War 7.1-3). kai ouk aphēsousin en soi lithon epi lithon — and they will not leave stone upon stone in you — the precise prediction of the temple's destruction confirmed by Josephus (War 7.1: "the Romans led by Titus demolished the city and the sanctuary to its foundations"). anth hōn ouk egnōs ton kairon tēs episkopēs sou — because you did not recognize the time of your visitation — kairos tēs episkopēs (the time of visitation — episkopē: inspection, oversight, the official visit of a dignitary or divine messenger; cf. Isaiah 10:3 LXX).
45: kai eiselthōn eis to hieron ērxato ekballein tous pōlountas — entering the temple he began to drive out those who were selling — hieron (the temple complex — the whole precinct, not the inner sanctuary naos). The sellers were in the Court of the Gentiles, selling ritually approved animals for sacrifice and exchanging foreign coins for the temple shekel required for the temple tax. Luke's version is the briefest of the four Gospel accounts: no mention of money-changers, doves, or buyers; simply the sellers driven out. The concision focuses on the prophetic act of cleansing rather than its commercial details.
46: legōn autois, Gegraptai, Kai estai ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs, hymeis de auton epoiēsate spēlaion lēstōn — saying to them: it is written: My house shall be a house of prayer; but you have made it a den of robbers — Isaiah 56:7 (LXX: ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs klēthēsetai pasi tois ethnesin — my house will be called a house of prayer for all nations; Luke omits "for all nations," Matthew and Mark retain it) + Jeremiah 7:11 (spēlaion lēstōn = den of robbers/bandits). The Jeremiah citation is from the Temple Sermon where YHWH condemned those who treated the temple as a refuge for their crimes. The "den of robbers" (spēlaion lēstōn) is not a comment on commercial exploitation alone but on using the temple as a hiding place — lēstēs means armed rebel/bandit, not petty thief.
47: kai ēn didaskōn to kath hēmeran en tō hierō — and he was teaching daily in the temple — the imperfect ēn didaskōn (was teaching, continued to teach) throughout the Jerusalem period; the temple becomes Jesus's regular teaching platform after the entry. hoi de archiereis kai hoi grammateis ezētoun auton apoplesai kai hoi prōtoi tou laou — but the chief priests and the scribes and the principal men of the people were seeking to destroy him — apōlesai (to destroy, kill) — the triple coalition: archiereis (chief priests — the Sadducean high-priestly families), grammateis (scribes — the Torah experts), and prōtoi tou laou (the principal men of the people — the lay aristocracy) form the Jerusalem establishment.
48: kai ouch heuriskon to ti poiēsōsin, ho laos gar hapas exekremato autou akouōn — and they could not find what they might do, for all the people were hanging on him listening — exekremato autou akouōn (were hanging on him listening — exkremamai: to hang on, be suspended from; perfect picture of rapt attention; the crowd is figuratively "hung" on Jesus's words, unable to be detached from him). This crowd-protection prevents immediate arrest; the authorities cannot act openly without provoking a popular uprising. The people's attentive hearing is the buffer between Jesus and his opponents through the Jerusalem teaching days.
